title: Jellyfish sting immediate shore response
summary: A shore-side response that prevents drowning, checks for severe allergy or systemic illness, avoids bare-hand tentacle contact and rubbing, and follows local species-specific first-aid advice.
content:
A shore-side response that prevents drowning, checks for severe allergy or systemic illness, avoids bare-hand tentacle contact and rubbing, and follows local species-specific first-aid advice.

Use the materials as a compact field kit. Work through the steps in order, pause whenever a warning or stop condition applies, and write down the outcome so another person can continue safely.
materials: clean container for rinsing method advised locally
steps: Help the person leave the water without exposing another rescuer to tentacles and prevent further contact with the animal or fragments.
Call 112 immediately for breathing difficulty, throat or face swelling, collapse, confusion, chest pain, widespread reaction, severe vomiting, a very young child, or a large sting area.

Without rubbing or using bare hands, remove visible tentacle material only by the locally recommended barrier method and follow current local guidance for rinsing and pain relief because species advice differs.

Record time, location, appearance of the animal if safely observed, symptoms, allergies, and treatment; seek medical review for severe or persistent pain, eye contact, infection signs, or worsening illness.
warnings: Do not rub, scrape aggressively, apply unverified chemicals, or use fresh water unless current local species guidance specifically advises it.
Do not re-enter the water to identify or capture the animal.
common_mistakes: Starting Jellyfish sting immediate shore response without first checking immediate danger and official instructions.
Failing to record time, location, responsible person, and unresolved risks for Jellyfish sting immediate shore response.
alternatives: Use a paper-only version of Jellyfish sting immediate shore response when power or connectivity is unavailable.
Assign one trained helper to simplify Jellyfish sting immediate shore response when the group is stressed or short-handed.
failure_signs: Conditions become more dangerous while carrying out Jellyfish sting immediate shore response.
Critical information cannot be confirmed or the assigned helper cannot continue Jellyfish sting immediate shore response safely.
when_not_to_use: Do not continue Jellyfish sting immediate shore response when it requires entering an unstable, contaminated, flooded, burning, electrically hazardous, or officially closed area.
Do not improvise medical, electrical, structural, or rescue actions beyond your training while using Jellyfish sting immediate shore response.
short_term: ['Within the first operational period, Jellyfish sting immediate shore response should produce a dated status, one responsible person, and a safe next action.', 'Repeat the essential check for Jellyfish sting immediate shore response whenever conditions, official advice, or available resources change.']
long_term: ['Include lessons from Jellyfish sting immediate shore response in the household or community emergency plan and resupply list.', 'Review the records from Jellyfish sting immediate shore response after the event and correct gaps before the next emergency.']
if_method_fails: ['If Jellyfish sting immediate shore response cannot be completed, move to the safest verified location, notify the responsible contact, and record what remains unresolved.', 'Use official emergency services or qualified specialists when the limits of Jellyfish sting immediate shore response are reached.']
environment_notes: ['Adapt Jellyfish sting immediate shore response to local terrain, weather, building type, transport access, language, disability needs, and official restrictions.', 'In Greece, use 112 for urgent emergencies and follow Civil Protection or local-authority instructions relevant to Jellyfish sting immediate shore response.']
